title: Automatic Metadata Extraction
concept: Using an LLM or heuristic rules to extract structured fields (title, entities, category) from raw text at ingest time.

exercises.prompt: Why did we learn to attach metadata (like source, author, and date) to text chunks in the last lesson?

options.text: It makes the raw text chunks smaller and easier to read.
It allows you to filter search results by specific attributes like date or author.
It completely replaces the need for vector embeddings.

exercises.explain: Metadata fields like author and date act as filters, allowing you to narrow down vector searches to only the most relevant documents.

cards.title: Labeling at Scale
cards.body: If you have ten thousand documents, you can't manually tag each one. Automatic extraction does this work for you in milliseconds, labeling every chunk so your search engine can find it later.

cards.title: Automatic Metadata Extraction
cards.body: Automatic Metadata Extraction uses LLMs or rule-based code to scan raw text chunks during ingestion and pull out key details like topics, dates, and named entities to save as structured fields.

cards.title: The Smart Mailroom
cards.body: Think of metadata extraction like an automated mailroom sorter. Instead of a human reading every letter to write down the sender and category, a smart scanner reads the text, extracts key fields, and stamps them on the envelope instantly.

cards.title: Extracting a News Profile
cards.body: When ingesting a news article, an LLM is prompted to output a JSON object containing the main organizations mentioned, the primary topic, and a generated summary title, turning messy text into clean data.

cards.title: Myth: You Always Need an LLM
cards.body: While LLMs are great for complex themes and sentiment, simple regex rules or keyword scripts are often faster and cheaper for extracting things like zip codes, phone numbers, or dates.

cards.title: Manual vs. Automatic
cards.body: Compare the process of manual tagging against automatic extraction at the moment data enters your system.

left.text: Slow, expensive, and inconsistent human labeling of every document.

right.text: Fast, scalable LLM or rule-based parsing at ingest time.

exercises.prompt: What is the best way to ensure an LLM extracts metadata in a reliable format that your database can read?

options.text: Ask the LLM to write a casual paragraph about the text.
Provide a structured JSON schema or use function calling to enforce the output shape.
Let the LLM invent its own tags and formats for every chunk.
Manually rewrite the LLM's output after every single extraction step.

exercises.explain: Using structured schemas like JSON Schema forces the LLM to output data in a reliable, predictable format that your code can easily parse and save to a database.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A hybrid approach using both rule-based code and LLMs is a valid way to extract metadata.
exercises.explain: You can use simple code rules (like regular expressions) to pull out predictable formats like dates, and use LLMs for subjective details like sentiment or main topics.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to specify when metadata should be extracted to keep search queries fast:
exercises.sentence: We extract structured fields at ingest time so the data is already organized when a user searches.
exercises.explain: Extracting metadata at ingest time ensures that all chunks are labeled and structured before they are stored in the database, saving time during live user searches.

exercises.prompt: Identify which output looks like it was generated by an automatic metadata extraction model processing a company report:

options.text: This report talks about Q3 earnings and mentions that Microsoft is doing well in the cloud division.
I found some files about Microsoft in the database. Should I open them for you now?

exercises.explain: Option B represents an AI extracting clean, structured, and structured metadata fields from the text input, whereas options A and C are just standard text or conversational responses.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer support RAG system. How does automatically extracting metadata like 'Product Category' and 'Software Version' at ingest time help solve the problem of retrieving outdated or irrelevant documents?

options.text: Applying strict database filters on the extracted metadata to narrow down the search space before running vector search.
Using the LLM to translate all chunks into a different language to make vector matching easier.
Discarding the vector embeddings entirely and relying only on keyword searches of the extracted tags.
Running a second LLM query on every single document in the database during the user's search query.

exercises.explain: Extracting metadata at ingest time allows you to apply strict database filters (like year >= 2023) before performing vector similarity search. This drastically reduces the search space and prevents the system from retrieving outdated, irrelevant chunks.
